test_id_1.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Pharmacy claims will initially be rejected for National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP)
Reject code "889" (Non-Matched Prescriber ID). This means the prescriber is not enrolled in NYS Medicaid.
• To override above rejection for the OOS prescription situations described above:
o In Field 439-E4 (Reason for Service Code): enter "PN" (Prescriber Consultation)
o In Field 441-E6 (Result of Service Code): enter applicable value ("1A", "1B", "1C", "1D", "1E", "1F",
"1G", "1H", "1J", "1K", "2A", "2B", "3A", "3B", "3C", "3D", "3E", "3F", "3G", "3H", "3J", "3K", "3M", "3N",
"4A")
o In Field 420-DK (Submission Clarification Code): enter "02" (Other Override)

Please note: The billing guidance above may not be used for claims or orders prescribed by an OOS licensed prescriber
that is treating more than one Medicaid member for more than a single instance of emergency care within a 180-day
period, or more than one Medicaid member for multiple instances of care when the services provided are more readily
available in another state within a 180-day period.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The NYS Medicaid Program Pharmacy Manual details that Medicaid does not cover Medicare Part D drugs for full-benefit dual-eligible members and sets payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims. It also requires Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to bill Medicare first and accept assignment for dual-eligible services, a point reinforced by policy guidance for specific services like immunizations.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from these individuals.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for immunization services provided to non-dual eligible members. Dual-eligible members must continue to access immunization services through Medicare. This implies Medicare is the primary payer for these specific services for dual-eligible individuals.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims for Medicare-covered services. They must also accept assignments for these services when provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of the pharmacys CMS-460 enrollment status.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Dual-eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare. This policy statement implies that Medicare is the primary payer for these specific services. The policy guidance is less specific than the manual regarding the broader obligation to bill Medicare first for all covered services.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance indicate that Medicaid does not cover certain services or drug classes for dual-eligible members when Medicare is the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

Both sources acknowledge that dual-eligible members access certain services, such as immunizations, through Medicare, indicating Medicare as the primary payer for those services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifies that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, while the policy guidance focuses on immunization services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].
The provider manual also details payment limits and prohibitions on billing for Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual explicitly states a federal law requirement for all Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignment for all Medicare-covered services for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

In contrast, the policy guidance specifically mentions immunization services and implies Medicare as the primary payer without detailing the broader billing obligation [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The NYS Medicaid Program's Pharmacy Manual clarifies that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members and sets payment limitations for Medicare/Medicaid claims. Policy updates reinforce that dual-eligible members access specific services like immunizations through Medicare, not Medicaid, and require the ordering provider's NPI on Medicaid claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must use their Part D plan or its exception and appeals process to obtain coverage. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Dual-eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for these immunization services. Medicaid Managed Care enrollees access immunization services through their health plans.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing Medicare enrollments must determine if their business situation requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim. Providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are also enrolled in Medicare. This policy is broader as it specifies a general identifier requirement on Medicaid claims.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance indicate that Medicaid does not cover certain pharmacy-related services or drug classes for dual-eligible members, directing them to Medicare for coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifically states that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members and sets payment limitations for Medicare/Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Policy updates reinforce that dual-eligible members access specific services like immunizations through Medicare, not Medicaid, and require the ordering providers NPI on Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10, New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1].

The provider manual focuses on the federal requirement for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to submit claims for Medicare-covered services for dual-eligibles, while the policy specifies a general identifier (NPI) required on Medicaid claims, which would apply when Medicaid is involved for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1].

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual outlines general policies for dual eligible pharmacy claims, stating that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D and that Medicare/Medicaid payments are considered payment in full. Recent policy updates reinforce this by providing specific examples, such as Medicaid not covering the RSV vaccine for dual eligible members.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. The total Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and must be accepted as payment in full, with no recovery of Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual eligible individuals.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must not bill for the provision of the Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) vaccine to New York State Medicaid members who are also enrolled in Medicare. This policy update provides a specific example of a service not covered by Medicaid for dual eligible members. This guidance is more specific than the general coverage limitations outlined in the provider manual.

similarities: Both the provider manual and the policy update address limitations on Medicaid coverage for services provided to dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual provides general guidance on Medicaids non-coverage of drugs under Medicare Part D and payment-in-full requirements for dual eligible members, whereas the policy update offers a specific example regarding the Respiratory Syncytial Virus vaccine [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1].

test_id_4.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Pharmacy claims will initially be rejected for National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP)
Reject code "889" (Non-Matched Prescriber ID). This means the prescriber is not enrolled in NYS Medicaid.
• To override above rejection for the OOS prescription situations described above:
o In Field 439-E4 (Reason for Service Code): enter "PN" (Prescriber Consultation)
o In Field 441-E6 (Result of Service Code): enter applicable value ("1A", "1B", "1C", "1D", "1E", "1F",
"1G", "1H", "1J", "1K", "2A", "2B", "3A", "3B", "3C", "3D", "3E", "3F", "3G", "3H", "3J", "3K", "3M", "3N",
"4A")
o In Field 420-DK (Submission Clarification Code): enter "02" (Other Override)

Please note: The billing guidance above may not be used for claims or orders prescribed by an OOS licensed prescriber
that is treating more than one Medicaid member for more than a single instance of emergency care within a 180-day
period, or more than one Medicaid member for multiple instances of care when the services provided are more readily
available in another state within a 180-day period.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, New York State Medicaid guidance specifies that total payments will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and generally does not cover drugs or services, such as those under Medicare Part D or specific immunizations like the RSV vaccine, for these members. Additionally, prescribers must be actively enrolled in NYS Medicaid with their National Provider Identifier (NPI) for pharmacy claims to be processed.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The total Medicare and Medicaid payment to a pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must not bill for the provision of the Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) vaccine to New York State Medicaid members who are also enrolled in Medicare. This policy provides a specific example of a non-covered service for dual-eligible members. The policy is more specific than the general guidance in the provider manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy claims will initially be rejected with National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) Reject code "889". This code indicates that the prescriber is not enrolled in New York State Medicaid. Override guidance is available for specific out-of-state prescription situations.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a New York State Medicaid provider, unless an exemption applies. The prescriber's National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required on the claim for payment processing. This policy statement aligns strongly with the provider manual's rejection codes.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid has limitations on coverage and payment for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1].

Both sources require prescribers to be enrolled in NYS Medicaid for pharmacy claims to be processed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

Both documents highlight the importance of proper prescriber identification for claim processing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual broadly states that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, while the policy update provides a specific example of a non-covered service, the RSV vaccine, for Medicare-enrolled NYS Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1].

The provider manual details specific National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) reject codes and override guidance for out-of-state prescriptions when a prescriber is not enrolled, whereas the policy update focuses on the general requirement for active prescriber enrollment and National Provider Identifier (NPI) submission for payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

answers.headline_summary: The NYS Medicaid Program Pharmacy Manual and recent Medicaid Updates clarify that Medicaid generally does not cover pharmacy services for dual eligible members that are covered by Medicare Part D or other Medicare benefits, such as immunizations. For covered services, the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment is considered payment in full, with specific prohibitions against balance billing for Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance, a principle reinforced by policy updates for specific services.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Dual eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. The combined Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, and providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from these individuals.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for immunization services provided to non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members must continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care enrollees will access immunization services through their health plans.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update clarify that Medicaid does not cover specific pharmacy-related services for dual eligible members, directing them to access these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifies that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for dual eligible members, whereas the policy update addresses Medicaid coverage for immunization services for this population [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

test_id_6.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, New York State Medicaid guidance establishes Medicare Part D as the primary payer for covered drugs, requiring members to enroll in a Part D plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid consistently functions as the payer of last resort, mandating providers to exhaust all other available third-party liability, including Medicare, before submitting claims to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual-eligible members, enrollment in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan is required to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an appropriate drug covered by the Part D plan.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, requiring all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, to be billed before Medicaid for dually enrolled members. This policy statement is broader, applying to all services for dual-eligibles, not just pharmacy.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Providers must pursue any available other coverage as the primary payer prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, as federal regulations require all other available resources to be used first. Through coordination of benefits, NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims.

similarities: Both guidance documents establish that Medicare is a primary payer for dual-eligible members, requiring it to be billed before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both the provider manual and policy guidance explicitly state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and require providers to bill all other available third-party coverage before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically details Medicare Part Ds role for pharmacy claims, stating Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance broadly states that Medicare must be billed first for dually enrolled members across all services, without specific mention of Part D or pharmacy claims [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications

in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.

Utilization Threshold

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Medicaid and provider manual guidance define Medicare Part D as the primary payer for full-benefit dual-eligible pharmacy claims, with Medicaid explicitly not covering drugs under Part D. Providers must determine other insurance coverage, including Medicare, and bill it first, as Medicaid serves as the payer of last resort.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicare members who are also Medicaid dual-eligible must enroll in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and prescribers should work together to find a Part D covered drug or use the plans exception and appeals process.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Incorrectly bypassing the primary plans responsibility for payment is considered inaccurate billing. This includes billing Medicare Part B for Part D services or vice versa. Such inaccurate billing may lead to audit recoveries.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims for Medicare-covered services. They must also accept assignments for these services when provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of the pharmacys CMS-460 enrollment status.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Dual-eligible members continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for immunization services provided to non-dual eligible members. This indicates Medicare as the primary billing point for dual-eligible immunization services.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: It is the providers responsibility to determine if a patient has other insurance coverage. Providers must also ascertain if that other insurance covers the service being billed. If other insurance covers the service, the provider must submit a claim to that carrier before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers, including pharmacies, are responsible for actively seeking Third-Party Liability enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients. They must bill timely and to the appropriate party. This includes billing commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid as applicable.

similarities: Both sources define Medicare Part D as the primary payer for dual-eligible pharmacy claims and emphasize the necessity of billing the primary plan correctly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary access and billing point for services provided to dual-eligible members, requiring pharmacies to bill Medicare for those services rather than Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

Both sources emphasize the providers responsibility to identify and bill other insurance, including Medicare, as the primary payer before billing Medicaid, which is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual broadly states that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, while the policy update focuses on the consequences of incorrectly bypassing primary plan responsibility for payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual states a general federal requirement for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for all Medicare-covered services for dual-eligible members, whereas the policy update specifically highlights that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10].

The provider manual explicitly states that providers must submit a claim to other insurance carriers prior to billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort, while the policy update broadly reminds providers of their responsibility to actively seek Third-Party Liability enrollment and bill the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid guidance establishes Medicare Part D as the primary payer for prescription drugs for full-benefit dual-eligible members, explicitly stating Medicaid does not cover drugs under Part D. Both the provider manual and policy emphasize that Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to exhaust all other available insurance benefits before billing Medicaid.

These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Members and their prescribers should work together to find an appropriate drug covered by the Part D plan or use the plan’s exception and appeals process.

This policy statement is broader and does not specifically mention Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage. If other insurance exists, providers must submit a claim to that carrier prior to billing Medicaid. Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort for covered services.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources to be used first. Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for coverable claims up to the allowed amount after other benefits are exhausted.

similarities: Both sources define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims by establishing Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both the provider manual and policy guidance outline the general principle that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other insurance first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, requiring their enrollment in a Part D plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

In contrast, the policy guidance for primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible claims is broader, focusing on Medicaid as the general payer of last resort without specific mention of Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.

• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, the provider manual specifies that Medicare Part D is the primary payer for prescription drugs covered under Part D, and Medicaid will not cover these drug classes. Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to exhaust all other available third-party coverage, including Medicare, before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicare members who are dual-eligible must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Providers must determine if a patient has other insurance and if the service is covered. If the service is covered or if coverage is unknown, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations require all other available resources to be used first.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

Both sources require providers to submit claims to other insurance carriers or third parties before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically states that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance provides a broader statement about Medicaids payer of last resort rule without explicitly detailing Medicare Part D drug coverage for dual-eligibles [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

answers.headline_summary: Medicaid guidance and provider manuals define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims by establishing Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other applicable insurance first. Specifically, for full-benefit dual-eligible members, Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D, making Part D the primary payer for those prescriptions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual-eligible Medicare members, enrollment in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan is required to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, making Part D the primary payer for these prescriptions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties, including Medicare, before billing NYS Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer and submit claims under the appropriate coverage benefit. This policy statement is broader than the provider manual, which specifically addresses Medicare Part.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance and must submit a claim to that carrier prior to billing Medicaid, even if unsure if the service is covered.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance establish that New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to determine if a patient has other insurance and to submit claims to those carriers before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources identify Medicare as an applicable third party that must be billed prior to Medicaid for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, making Part D the primary payer for these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance provides a broader instruction for providers to bill all applicable third parties, including Medicare, before billing NYS Medicaid, without specific detail on Medicare Part D drug classes [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.
