provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual and policy guidance consistently prohibit providers from seeking to recover Medicare cost-sharing amounts from dually eligible individuals. The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B deductibles and coinsurance for pharmacy providers, while the policy guidance focuses on Medicare Part C co-payments and co-insurance for general providers.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy providers offering a Medicare Part B benefit are prohibited from seeking to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. This rule applies specifically to Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full by the pharmacy provider.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers offering a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dually eligible individuals. Providers are required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from attempting to recover Medicare cost-sharing amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources state that providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B deductibles and coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy guidance applies to general providers of a Medicare Part C benefit and focuses on co-payment and co-insurance amounts [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Both NYS Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance state that providers cannot bill dual-eligible members for Medicare Part B or Part C deductibles, co-insurance, or co-payments, requiring acceptance of Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full. Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, covering certain Medicare Part B cost-sharing amounts for dually enrolled members.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals. Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. This regulation ensures that dual-eligible members are not billed for these specific Medicare Part B cost-sharing amounts.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider must accept Medicare Part C and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full. The member may not be billed for any Part C co-payment or co-insurance not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from billing dual-eligible members for Medicare cost-sharing amounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B deductibles and coinsurance for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

In contrast, the policy guidance focuses on Medicare Part C co-payment and co-insurance amounts for providers of a Medicare Part C benefit [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

test_id_13.query_content: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.

pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.

process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.

answers.headline_summary: Both the provider manual and Medicaid policy prohibit providers from collecting Medicare Part B or Part C co-payments and deductibles directly from dual-eligible members. For general Medicaid co-payments, the provider manual states that providers cannot refuse services to members unable to pay, while policy indicates Medicaid may reimburse Medicare Part B coinsurance and deductibles for dual-eligible members in certain contexts.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. Additionally, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid members ability to pay co-payments. Providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment. This applies to co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers a telehealth encounter, NYS Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible. This reimbursement occurs to the extent permitted by NYS law. Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort, meaning other sources like Medicare must be billed first.

similarities: Both the provider manual and Medicaid policy prohibit providers from directly collecting Medicare co-payment or coinsurance amounts from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources emphasize that the Medicare and Medicaid payments, when combined, must be accepted as payment in full for services provided to dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses the prohibition on collecting Medicare Part B deductibles and coinsurance from dual-eligible members for pharmacy services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Medicaid policy focuses on the prohibition of collecting Medicare Part C co-payments and coinsurance directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual outlines a general obligation for pharmacy providers not to refuse services to Medicaid members, including dual-eligibles, who cannot afford co-payments for drugs and medical supplies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Medicaid policy details that NYS Medicaid will reimburse Medicare Part B coinsurance and deductibles for dual-eligible members in specific scenarios, such as telehealth encounters [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, both Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance prohibit providers from billing these individuals for Medicare Part B or Part C co-payments and deductibles. Medicaid generally acts as the payer of last resort, covering specific services or portions of costs after Medicare, but explicitly excludes coverage for most Medicare Part D drugs for full-benefit dual-eligible members.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: A pharmacy provider offering a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dually eligible individuals. The combined Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from dually eligible individuals. The provider must accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Exceptions include specific drug classes excluded by law, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization requirements.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, meaning all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed before Medicaid. For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers a telehealth encounter, NYS Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by state law. This policy statement is broader than the provider manual, which focuses specifically on drug coverage.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from billing dual-eligible members for Medicare co-payments or deductibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources emphasize that providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B benefits regarding billing prohibitions for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
Policy guidance extends the billing prohibition to providers of Medicare Part C benefits for co-payments and co-insurance [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual details Medicaids exclusion of most Medicare Part D drug classes for full-benefit dual-eligible members, with specific exceptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Policy guidance broadly states Medicaids role as the payer of last resort and provides an example of reimbursing Medicare Part B coinsurance and deductibles for telehealth encounters [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

provider_manual.text:
Since co-payments paid or incurred can be used toward satisfying the spend-down (overage)
in the following month, itemized bills or receipts for co-payments should be provided to members when
requested.
• There is a maximum amount per Medicaid member for all co-payments incurred per year and is calculated
on a quarterly basis. The co-payment year starts April 1 and ends March 31. When a member reaches the
quarterly co-pay maximum, they will receive a letter confirming the date on which the co-pay maximum
was met and exempting the member from a co-payment until the end of the current co-payment quarter.
• Co-payment amounts are as follows:
o $3.00 for non-preferred Brand Name Drugs.
o $1.00 for Generic Drugs, preferred Brand Name Drugs, and Brand Drugs included in the Brand Less
Than Generic Drugs Program.
o $0.50 for Non-Prescription (over the counter) Drugs.
o $1.00 for Medical/Sickroom Supplies.
• Co-payment is not required for certain members and service categories which include:

o Family planning (birth control) services including birth control pills, emergency contraception, and
condoms.

answers.headline_summary: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from billing dual-eligible members for Medicare Part B or Part C co-payments and coinsurance. Providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for these services.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. This guidance specifically applies to Medicare Part B benefits.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. Dual-eligible members may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from billing dual-eligible members for Medicare Part B or Part C co-payments and coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services provided to dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B benefits, while the policy guidance focuses on providers of Medicare Part C benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

provider_manual.text:
Additional information regarding the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Program and residential health care
facilities may be accessed at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/.
See Section 4.0 for Long Term Care Pharmacy Short Cycle Billing information.
Items Provided by Child (Foster) Care Agencies
Please see this link for items provided by Childcare Agencies:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/carveout.htm.
OMH Residential Treatment Facility Prescription Drug Carve-Out
Reimbursement of prescription drugs for children and youth between the ages of five and twenty-one who are
residents of the Office of Mental Health (OMH) Residential Treatment Facilities (RTF) is a NYRx benefit and billed
directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy.
Physician administered drugs, OTC drugs, medical supplies, immunization services (vaccines and their administration),
nutritional supplies, sick room supplies, adult diapers, and durable medical equipment (DME) are not carved out of the
RTF rate and remain the responsibility of the facility.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid guidance clarifies that while Medicaid generally does not cover drugs covered by Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members, it *does* cover specific drug classes legally excluded from Part D. Policy guidance, however, has previously stated that Medicaid will not cover a medication if the primary insurer does not, highlighting a difference in approach for Part D-excluded drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs if the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization requirements.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: An older policy states that if the primary insurer, such as Medicare Part D, does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Providers must resolve any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, with the third party before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work with the Part D plan to find an appropriate covered drug. If needed, they should use the Part D plans exception and appeals process to obtain coverage.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers are instructed to bill Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan for prescription drugs covered by Part D. This applies when a member has Medicare and requires coverage for these drugs.

similarities: Both sources emphasize that claims for dual-eligible members must adhere to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization, and issues with the primary payer must be resolved first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid is not the primary payer for drugs generally covered by Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid *will* cover specific drug classes legally excluded from Medicare Part D, such as certain prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

An older policy, however, broadly states that Medicaid will *not* cover a medication if the primary insurer, such as Medicare Part D, does not cover it [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual details the process for dual-eligible members to work with their Part D plan, including using exception and appeals processes for coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The policy guidance referenced is older than the provider manual guidance. The provider manual, effective October 2025, provides more recent and specific instructions regarding Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid guidance specifies that Medicaid will only cover prescription drugs for Medicare Part D dual-eligible members if the drug class is legally excluded from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. Generally, Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, covering only medications not covered by Medicare Part D, and claims for dual-eligible members are subject to Medicaid rules including prior authorization.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If the primary insurer, Medicare Part D, does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Providers must bill Medicare Part D for prescription drugs for members with Medicare. This policy statement is broader and less specific regarding drug classes excluded by law.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Members and prescribers should use the Part D plans exception and appeals process for non-formulary drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party, Medicare Part D, before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid. This policy statement strongly aligns with the principle of Medicare Part D as the primary payer.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer for dual-eligible members, covering drugs only after Medicare Part D has been addressed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both sources indicate that claims for dual-eligible members are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization, once Medicare Part D processes are exhausted or if the drug is excluded from Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly lists examples of drug classes that Medicaid may cover because they are legally excluded from Medicare Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update states more broadly that if Medicare Part D does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, without detailing specific Part D-excluded drug classes [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual specifies that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain Medicaid coverage and outlines the use of Part Ds exception and appeals process for non-formulary drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The policy guidance from July 2020 is broader and less specific regarding the types of drugs legally excluded from Medicare Part D that Medicaid may cover, compared to the more detailed and recent provider manual.

Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual clarifies that Medicaid covers specific drug classes legally excluded from Medicare Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs, subject to Medicaid's own prior authorization rules. Conversely, policy guidance generally mandates that Medicare Part D is the primary payer for dual-eligible members' prescription drugs, requiring providers to bill Part D first and noting that Medicaid typically will not cover claims for medications not covered by Part.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid pays for drugs from its List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans. This includes select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Policy guidance generally states that if the primary insurer, Medicare Part D, does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. This policy is broader and does not specifically address drug classes legally excluded from Part D. Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Full-benefit dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for these members. Members and prescribers must work to find a Part D covered drug or use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals process.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a member has Medicare, providers will bill Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plan for prescription drugs. If a member has Medicare Parts A or B but is not enrolled in Part D, providers will bill the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition program. This process initiates Part D enrollment and facilitates payment.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicare Part D is the primary payer for prescription drugs for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be addressed for claims involving dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual specifically identifies certain drug classes, such as select prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs, as covered by Medicaid when legally excluded from Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

In contrast, the policy guidance offers a broader statement that Medicaid generally will not cover a medication if the primary insurer, Medicare Part D, does not cover it, without detailing specific exclusions [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The policy guidance explicitly mentions billing the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition program for members with Medicare Parts A or B not enrolled in Part D to initiate enrollment and facilitate payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid guidance from the provider manual states that Medicaid covers certain drug classes legally excluded by law from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members. However, policy guidance indicates that Medicaid will not cover claims for medications not covered by the primary insurer, Medicare Part.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans. Examples of these excluded drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for other prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If the primary insurer, Medicare Part D, does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Providers are instructed to bill Medicare Part D for prescription drugs. This policy guidance is broader and does not specify drug classes that Medicaid might cover.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs already covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work with the Part D plan to obtain coverage. They should also use the Part D plans exception and appeals process if necessary.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a member has Medicare, providers must bill Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) plan for prescription drugs. Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting the claim to Medicaid. This policy guidance is consistent with the provider manuals general approach.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance address Medicaids coverage for drugs not covered by Medicare Part.

Both documents state that Medicare Part D is the primary payer for prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, and Medicaid does not cover drug classes already covered by Part D [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual specifies that Medicaid covers certain drug classes legally excluded from Medicare Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs, while the policy states more broadly that Medicaid will not cover claims for medications not covered by the primary insurer [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual explicitly states that members and prescribers must use the Part D plans exception and appeals process for coverage, a detail not explicitly mentioned in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

OMH Residential Treatment Facility Prescription Drug Carve-Out
Reimbursement of prescription drugs for children and youth between the ages of five and twenty-one who are
residents of the Office of Mental Health (OMH) Residential Treatment Facilities (RTF) is a NYRx benefit and billed
directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy.

test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Fee-For-Service Reminders from the Bureau of Dental Review
The Need to Bill Third-Party Insurers
Third-party insurers, including Medicare, provide reimbursement for various dental procedures. Since
Medicaid is the payer of last resort, the provider must bill the member’s third-party payers prior to requesting
payment from Medicaid. If the third party is a commercial plan, Medicaid will reimburse the difference only if
the total third-party payment(s) is (are) less than the lesser of the provider’s fee charged to the general public
or the fee developed by New York State Department of Health (DOH) for the specific procedure code. If the
third party is a Medicare Advantage plan, Medicaid will reimburse eighty-five percent (85%) of the patient
responsibility. Prior to initiating treatment which has been approved by a third-party insurance plan, the
provider should obtain a prior approval from Medicaid to ensure that the treatment plan falls within the current
guidelines of the Medicaid Program. Failure to do so may result in the denial of Medicaid benefits for these
services.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid policy and provider manual guidelines consistently require pharmacies to bill Medicare before Medicaid for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must maximize Medicare benefits and receive payment information from Medicare prior to submitting claims to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. This policy is broader, encompassing all third parties, not just Medicare.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Providers must submit a claim to other insurance carriers prior to billing Medicaid. This is because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if the service is covered.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and NYS Medicaid policy explicitly state that other insurance, including Medicare, must be billed before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources consistently identify Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring providers to exhaust other available benefits first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically details the requirement for pharmacies to participate in the Medicare Program and to bill Medicare Parts A, B, and D first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update emphasizes that federal regulations mandate the use of all other available resources before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.

unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the

2.4 Pharmacy Services Billing Instructions
This subsection of the Billing Guidelines covers the specific NYS Medicaid billing requirements for Pharmacies. Although
the instructions that follow are based on the eMedNY-000301 paper claim form, they are also intended as a guideline
for electronic billers to find out what information they need to provide in their claims. For further electronic claim
submission information, refer to the NCPDP D.0 Request and Response Companion Guide which is available at
www.emedny.org by clicking: NCPDP D.0 Companion Guide.
It is important that providers adhere to the instructions outlined below. Claims that do not conform to the eMedNY
requirements as described throughout this document may be rejected, pended, or denied.

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid for covered services, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Providers are responsible for maximizing Medicare benefits and following specific Medicare billing procedures before submitting claims to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacies are responsible for billing the appropriate Medicare benefit and notifying members and prescribers of any required authorizations or changes.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. This is because Medicaid is the payer of last resort, as required by federal law. For dually enrolled members, if Medicare covers a telehealth encounter, NYS Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance clearly state that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual specifies that pharmacies must bill using the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or National Drug Code (NDC) as required by the Medicare Plan, while the policy update provides a general statement about Medicaid being the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Fee-For-Service Reminders from the Bureau of Dental Review
The Need to Bill Third-Party Insurers
Third-party insurers, including Medicare, provide reimbursement for various dental procedures. Since
Medicaid is the payer of last resort, the provider must bill the member’s third-party payers prior to requesting
payment from Medicaid. If the third party is a commercial plan, Medicaid will reimburse the difference only if
the total third-party payment(s) is (are) less than the lesser of the provider’s fee charged to the general public
or the fee developed by New York State Department of Health (DOH) for the specific procedure code. If the
third party is a Medicare Advantage plan, Medicaid will reimburse eighty-five percent (85%) of the patient
responsibility. Prior to initiating treatment which has been approved by a third-party insurance plan, the
provider should obtain a prior approval from Medicaid to ensure that the treatment plan falls within the current
guidelines of the Medicaid Program. Failure to do so may result in the denial of Medicaid benefits for these
services.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

provider_manual.text:
Amount Charged (Field 22)
Enter the total amount charged for each service rendered. The amount must not exceed the provider's usual and
customary charge.
Medicare Co-Insurance (Field 23)
When applicable, enter the Medicare co-insurance amount for this drug/supply.
NOTES:
Fields 23, 23A, 23B, and 23C are only applicable if the member is also a Medicare beneficiary.
It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether Medicare covers the service being billed. If the
service is covered or if the provider does not know if the service is covered, the provider must first submit a
claim to Medicare, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.
If the provider knows that the service rendered is not covered by Medicare, enter zero in field 23C.
Medicare Deductible (Field 23A)
When applicable, enter the Medicare deductible amount for this drug/supply.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid requires pharmacies to bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must maximize Medicare benefits and can only bill Medicaid after receiving payment information from Medicare.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Providers must also bill by the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing New York State Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to New York State Medicaid. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, whether medical or pharmacy.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a service is covered by Medicare or if the provider does not know if the service is covered, the provider must first submit a claim to Medicare. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. It is the providers responsibility to determine Medicare coverage for the service being billed.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before New York State Medicaid considers payment. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing New York State Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update require that services covered by other payers, including Medicare, must be billed before New York State Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents state that New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources highlight the providers responsibility to bill Medicare first for covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifies that pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare, detailing specific billing procedures like HCPCS or NDC [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing New York State Medicaid, without specifying the receipt of payment information [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual explicitly states it is the providers responsibility to determine if Medicare covers a service or to bill Medicare if coverage is unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].

The policy update grounds the "payer of last resort" rule in federal regulations, which is not explicitly mentioned in the provider manual chunks [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid requires pharmacies to bill Medicare before Medicaid for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must ensure Medicare benefits are maximized and are responsible for coordinating with the appropriate primary payer before submitting claims to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacies must maximize Medicare benefits and bill Medicare first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. Providers can bill Medicaid only after receiving payment information from Medicare. It is the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. This requirement applies to dual-eligible enrollees.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual specifies that pharmacies must wait for payment information from Medicare before billing Medicaid, detailing the operational sequence [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance broadly states that all other reimbursement sources must be exhausted before billing Medicaid, without detailing the specific information required from Medicare [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
