policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Amount Charged (Field 22)
Enter the total amount charged for each service rendered. The amount must not exceed the provider's usual and
customary charge.
Medicare Co-Insurance (Field 23)
When applicable, enter the Medicare co-insurance amount for this drug/supply.
NOTES:
Fields 23, 23A, 23B, and 23C are only applicable if the member is also a Medicare beneficiary.
It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether Medicare covers the service being billed. If the
service is covered or if the provider does not know if the service is covered, the provider must first submit a
claim to Medicare, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.
If the provider knows that the service rendered is not covered by Medicare, enter zero in field 23C.
Medicare Deductible (Field 23A)
When applicable, enter the Medicare deductible amount for this drug/supply.

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacies must always bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid consistently functions as the payer of last resort. Providers are responsible for maximizing Medicare benefits and coordinating with the appropriate primary payer before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after receiving payment information from Medicare.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dually enrolled members.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a service is covered by Medicare, or if the provider does not know if it is covered, the provider must first submit a claim to Medicare. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether Medicare covers the service being billed.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy updates consistently state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both sources require that Medicare be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both programs for dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both documents emphasize the providers responsibility to maximize Medicare benefits before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after receiving payment information from Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual specifies that a claim must be submitted to Medicare first even if the provider does not know if the service is covered [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].

The policy update highlights that the requirement for Medicaid to be the payer of last resort is pursuant to federal law [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure, even if Medicaid would typically require it. However, generally, any prior authorization requirements for claims involving a third party must be resolved with that primary payer before submitting the claim to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service or procedure. This exemption applies even if the procedure would have typically required Medicaid prior approval.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For claims involving a third party, such as Medicare, any prior authorization requirements must be resolved with that third party. This resolution must occur prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update address prior authorization requirements for claims where Medicaid is the secondary payer after a third party [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that prior approval is not required for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update, however, broadly states that all prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
answers.caveats: The policy update provides a broader statement on resolving prior authorization with third parties before Medicaid submission, while the provider manual offers a specific exemption for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims when Medicare has already paid.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required by Medicaid if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure. However, providers are consistently required to pursue any available other coverage, including Medicare, prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, resolving any third-party prior authorization requirements first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required by Medicaid. This applies even if Medicaid would have otherwise required prior approval for the service or procedure. The exemption is valid if Medicare has already approved and paid for the service or procedure.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party. This resolution must occur prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. This policy statement is broader, emphasizing resolution with the third party before Medicaid submission, rather than explicitly waiving Medicaid prior approval after Medicare payment.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal and Medicaid regulations mandate that providers pursue any available other coverage. This action must occur prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. The primary payor must pay first for the Medicaid members health benefits.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Federal and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. The primary payor must pay first for the Medicaid members health benefits. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the responsibility of the primary payor.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize that providers must resolve prior authorization requirements with third parties, such as Medicare, before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both sources consistently state that federal and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage, including Medicare, as the primary payor before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both documents clarify that the primary payors responsibility is not overcome by a failure to obtain prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid prior approval is not required for crossover claims if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance is broader, stating that all claim issues, including prior authorization, must be resolved with the third party before submitting to Medicaid, without explicitly waiving Medicaids own prior approval requirement after Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
answers.caveats: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more recent and specific guidance regarding the waiver of Medicaid prior approval for crossover claims after Medicare payment, while the policy documents are older, with the newest effective date being November 1, 2021.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure, even if Medicaid would typically require it. This aligns with the general principle that third-party payers must be billed first and their prior authorization requirements resolved before submitting claims to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure. This applies even if Medicaid would typically require prior approval for that specific procedure.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting the claim to Medicaid. This guidance emphasizes the general principle of resolving prior authorization with the primary payer first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Failure to obtain prior authorization from the primary payer does not overcome the responsibility of that primary payer.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Federal regulations mandate that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to pursue other coverage first before submitting a claim to Medicaid. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the primary payers responsibility.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources state that providers must pursue other coverage first and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the primary payers responsibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that prior approval is not required for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service or procedure [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update provides a broader statement that all claim issues, including prior authorization, must be resolved with the third party before submitting to Medicaid, without specifically addressing the waiver of Medicaid prior approval after Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has approved and paid for a service or procedure, even if Medicaid would typically require it. Generally, any prior authorization requirements from the primary third-party payer must be resolved before submitting a claim to Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has approved and paid for a service or procedure. This applies even if Medicaid would have ordinarily required prior approval for that service.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. This includes situations involving Medicare as the primary payer.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal regulations mandate that all available resources must be utilized before Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not negate the responsibility of the primary payer.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payer.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources state that providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both documents clarify that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both the provider manual and policy agree that prior authorization requirements with the third party must be resolved before submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual specifically states that prior approval is not required for Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims if Medicare has already approved and paid for a service, even if Medicaid would typically require it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party prior to Medicaid submission, without explicitly detailing the waiver of Medicaid prior authorization for Medicare-paid services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
answers.caveats: The policy guidance on prior authorization for crossover claims is broader, stating that all claim issues, including prior authorization, must be resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission, whereas the provider manual offers a specific exception for Medicare-approved and paid services.

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid requires providers to bill all other liable third parties before submitting claims to Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on claims, with specific operational codes used depending on the claim's relation to other coverage, such as Workers' Compensation, and Medicaid will pay the lesser of the patient responsibility or the Medicaid fee.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the claim, if applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer using Eligibility Clarification Code “2” in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of benefits, NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid coverable claims, up to the allowed amount. This policy is broader as it applies to all NYS Medicaid providers, while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific details.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other liable third parties before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifies operational details for pharmacy claims, such as utilizing Workers’ Compensation for work-related injuries and using an Eligibility Clarification Code for unrelated drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. In contrast, the policy update provides a broader overview of Coordination of Benefits requirements for all NYS Medicaid providers and mentions federal regulations [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.

NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid is consistently designated as the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill all other applicable third-party payers before submitting claims to Medicaid. Specific billing instructions apply for different COB scenarios, including Workers' Compensation cases and the use of particular eligibility clarification codes for claims where NYRx may be primary for unrelated conditions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the claim. For work-related injuries, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer using Eligibility Clarification Code "2" in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used first. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both documents state that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both require providers to bill all other applicable third-party payers before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Workers’ Compensation cases, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx may be primary for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update emphasizes that federal regulations require the use of all other available resources before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The policy update also clarifies that NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted claims up to the allowed amount [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

test_id_34.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid requires providers to bill all applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. For specific scenarios like Workers' Compensation, coverage must be utilized first, with specific codes used for claims unrelated to the work injury.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the claim, if applicable. For members with an open Workers' Compensation case, that coverage must be utilized before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer using Eligibility Clarification Code '2' in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used first. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits and always ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper coordination of benefits.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other liable third parties first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual offers specific guidance for Workers' Compensation cases, including an eligibility clarification code for claims unrelated to the work injury [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update provides a broader, general instruction for all NYS Medicaid providers to coordinate benefits with any applicable third party [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

test_id_36.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists' responsibilities for missing information depend on the type of data. For missing patient demographics like address, age, or CIN, pharmacists are not required to enter this information if it is readily available in pharmacy records or the prescription is deemed legitimate. For situations involving unknown prescribers or unavailable prescriber verification in emergencies, pharmacists must make a good faith effort to verify the prescription's validity and the prescriber's identity, using professional judgment and ensuring follow-up.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a Medicaid members address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information on the prescription. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the person, or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Prescriptions for controlled substances must meet Article 33 of the Public Health Law, and pharmacists are only permitted to add or change certain information.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy states that pharmacies must use professional judgment to fill prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if they were written during an emergency department visit and the assigned Primary Care Provider is unavailable to verify them. The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned Primary Care Provider the next business day regarding these prescriptions. This policy is a partial match as it addresses prescriber verification in emergency scenarios rather than missing patient demographics.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. This effort ensures the legitimacy of the order. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may require authentication.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy states that the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment when filling prescriptions from an emergency department visit if the assigned Primary Care Provider is unavailable for verification. The pharmacy must contact the assigned Primary Care Provider the next business day regarding these prescriptions. This policy strongly matches the need for pharmacist action when prescriber verification is not immediately possible.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to ensure the legitimacy of prescriptions when information or verification is not immediately available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both documents require pharmacists to exercise professional judgment in situations where standard information or verification is missing or unavailable [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses the pharmacists discretion regarding missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN, and notes specific rules for controlled substances [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses on pharmacist responsibilities for prescriptions originating from emergency department visits when the assigned Primary Care Provider is unavailable for verification, requiring follow-up contact the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
