policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have specific responsibilities when prescriber information or action is incomplete. This includes making a good faith effort to verify an unknown prescribers identity for telephone orders and, in certain situations, initiating a 72-hour emergency supply prior authorization if the prescriber has not obtained a required prior authorization for a non-preferred drug and is unreachable.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For telephone orders, pharmacists are responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the prescribers identity. This effort is required when the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Pharmacists must also verify the validity of the prescription in such circumstances.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If a pharmacist cannot reach the prescriber and a prior authorization has not been obtained for a non-preferred drug, the pharmacist may initiate a prior authorization for a 72-hour emergency supply. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit prior authorizations or enter agreements to effectuate them on a prescribers behalf. An exception applies if the pharmacist is employed by the prescriber.

differences: The provider manual specifies the pharmacists responsibility to verify the identity and validity of a prescription for unknown prescribers in the context of telephone orders. [New York State Medicaid Program:

The policy update details the pharmacists ability to initiate a 72-hour emergency supply prior authorization when a prescriber is unreachable and has not obtained a required prior authorization for a non-preferred drug.

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have specific responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing. This includes determining if missing patient demographic data is readily available or if a prescription is legitimate, and initiating emergency prior authorization for non-preferred drugs if the prescriber cannot be reached.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists are not required to enter missing Medicaid member address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) on a prescription. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the requester, or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Prescriptions for controlled substances must meet Article 33 of the Public Health Law, and pharmacists are permitted to add or change only certain information on these prescriptions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization (PA) and it is missing, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber. If the prescriber cannot be reached, the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit PAs or enter agreements to effectuate PAs on a prescribers behalf, unless employed by the prescriber.

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have specific responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, including verifying prescription legitimacy and prescriber identity. While not required to enter certain missing patient demographics if readily available or the prescription is legitimate, pharmacists must make a good faith effort to verify unknown prescribers and may initiate emergency prior authorizations if a prescriber is unreachable.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists are not required to enter a Medicaid members missing address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) on a prescription. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the person, or is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For controlled substances, pharmacists are only permitted to add or change specific information according to New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The provided policy information does not directly address the pharmacists responsibility for missing patient demographic information on a prescription.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists are responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of a prescription. This includes verifying the prescribers identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If a pharmacist cannot reach the prescriber for a non-preferred drug requiring prior authorization, they may initiate a prior authorization for a 72-hour emergency supply. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit for prior authorization or enter agreements to effectuate prior authorizations on the prescriber’s behalf, except for this emergency supply or when employed by a prescriber.

similarities: Both documents outline specific responsibilities for pharmacists when information or action from other parties, such as patients or prescribers, is not immediately complete or available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual specifies when pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient demographic information, such as address, age, or Client Identification Number, if it is readily available or the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses on the pharmacist's ability to initiate a 72-hour emergency prior authorization supply when a prescriber is unreachable for a non-preferred drug, clarifying that pharmacists generally cannot submit prior authorizations on a prescriber's behalf [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The provider manual addresses the pharmacists responsibility to verify the identity of an unknown prescriber, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The policy update primarily addresses prior authorization procedures for non-preferred drugs when a prescriber is unreachable, rather than directly detailing responsibilities for missing patient or prescriber information on the prescription itself. The provider manual is more up-to-date and provides more specific guidance on handling missing patient demographic information and verifying prescriber identity.

test_id_40.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.

policy.text:
The supervising
pharmacist must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid. For additional information, providers should refer to the NYRx,
The NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program Pharmacy Manual – Policy Guidelines, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
As a NYS Medicaid provider, the supervising pharmacist is responsible for fulfilling the duties outlined in the
“Duties of the Provider” section of the New York State Medicaid Program: Information for All Providers –
General Policy document, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/PDFS/
Information_for_All_Providers-General_Policy.pdf. This includes adherence to all federal and NYS laws, rules
and regulations, and policies that govern the NYS Medicaid program, including NYS pharmacy law.
Responsibility for conformance with all laws and regulations applicable to the conduct of a pharmacy is placed
upon the ownership of the pharmacy and upon a licensed pharmacist who is designated by the owner as the
supervising pharmacist (i.e., pharmacist in charge)*.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have specific responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, including conditions under which missing patient demographic data does not need to be entered if the prescription is legitimate. They must also make a good faith effort to verify the identity of unknown prescribers to ensure prescription validity.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists are not required to enter missing Medicaid member address, age, or CIN on a prescription. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person. Alternatively, this applies if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists are responsible for ensuring that prescription claims are true, accurate, and for a legitimate medical purpose. This policy statement is broader and less specific than the provider manuals guidance on missing patient demographic data. It establishes an overarching requirement for claim legitimacy, which implicitly covers the need for legitimate prescriptions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists are responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of a prescription. This effort includes verifying the prescribers identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. This ensures the prescription is legitimate before dispensing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists must ensure that prescription claims submitted for payment are true, accurate, and for a legitimate medical purpose. This policy is broader and less specific than the provider manuals detailed instruction on verifying unknown prescribers. It sets a general standard for claim legitimacy, which supports the need for prescriber verification.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the importance of legitimate prescriptions and claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8].

The policy's general requirement for legitimate medical purposes supports the provider manual's specific instructions for verifying prescription validity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual offers specific operational guidance on when pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient demographic information, provided certain conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual explicitly details the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify an unknown prescriber's identity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy provides a broader, overarching responsibility for pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists to ensure claims are true, accurate, and for a legitimate medical purpose, without detailing specific verification steps for missing information [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. This exclusion applies generally to drugs used for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, and specifically to their inclusion in compounded medications. The exclusion is pursuant to Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs. They also cannot be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss, as specified by Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs. They also cannot be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs for weight loss, pursuant to Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide reimbursement for certain drugs. This includes drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is in accordance with Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for certain drugs. This includes drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This is as per Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications such as weight loss, referencing Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both the provider manual and policy update directly state that NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, citing Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.

additives to a commercially available product.

code for the dosage form.

members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Yes, drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. This exclusion applies to both general drug coverage and compounded medications, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds are not permitted to contain excluded drugs. This includes drugs made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, such as those for weight loss, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). Compounds also cannot be made for cosmetic purposes or sexual dysfunction.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications must not include excluded drugs. They also cannot be prepared for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, such as weight loss, according to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This also applies to drugs for sexual dysfunction or cosmetic purposes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used to treat anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This policy applies unless the cost of drugs is included in a facilitys all-inclusive rate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs found on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This policy applies unless the cost of drugs is included in a facilitys all-inclusive rate.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update specify that compounded drugs for weight loss are excluded from Medicaid reimbursement under the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources explicitly state that drugs for weight loss are not reimbursable by Medicaid, citing the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.

Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.

reasons.

a commercially available product.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid generally excludes drugs used for the treatment of weight loss from reimbursement, in accordance with Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This exclusion also applies to compounded drugs made for weight loss indications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded drugs may not contain excluded drugs. They also cannot be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This includes, but is not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded drugs are prohibited from containing excluded drugs. They also cannot be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, as outlined in the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This specifically includes drugs for weight loss.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used to treat anorexia. This exclusion also applies to drugs for weight loss or weight gain. This policy is pursuant to Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia. Similarly, drugs for weight loss or weight gain are not reimbursable. This is in accordance with Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that compounded drugs for weight loss are excluded from coverage, referencing Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, pursuant to Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used to treat anorexia. This also applies to drugs for weight loss or weight gain. This is in accordance with policy and/or state or federal legislation, specifically the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid generally excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement, as per federal Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This exclusion also specifically applies to compounded drugs made for weight loss indications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded drugs may not be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss. These exclusions are specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded drugs may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss. This policy is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This policy is pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This means such drugs are not covered for these specific indications.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This policy applies unless the cost of drugs is included in a facilitys all-inclusive rate.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy updates state that compounded drugs for weight loss are excluded from NYS Medicaid reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both documents cite Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs for weight loss and compounded drugs for weight loss indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: No material differences were identified between the provider manual and policy updates regarding the exclusion of weight loss drugs or compounded drugs for weight loss indications.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.

The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.

pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs based on their acquisition cost plus a professional dispensing fee. To avoid duplicate discounts, 340B claims require specific claim-level identifiers and must be submitted with the 340B acquisition cost in the ingredient cost field and the lowest net charge to cash customers in the usual and customary cost field.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers. These identifiers prevent federal duplicate discounts, where manufacturers provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drugs. This ensures that rebates are not collected for these drugs. Covered entities are responsible for reimbursing manufacturers 340B discounts if rebates are received due to incorrect or missing identifiers.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at their 340B Acquisition Cost. A professional dispensing fee of $10.18 is added to this cost. However, claims will pay at the pharmacys Usual and Customary pricing if it is lower than the drug ingredient cost plus the dispensing fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims must be submitted at actual acquisition cost. This applies when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor. The policy does not explicitly mention a professional dispensing fee or Usual and Customary pricing in this context.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields. These include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for primary claims. The Ingredient Cost Submitted must be the 340B Acquisition Cost, and the Usual and Customary Cost must be the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format require specific fields. These fields include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for Medicaid Primary claims. The Ingredient Cost Submitted must be the 340B Acquisition Cost, and the Usual and Customary Cost must be the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers for both primary and secondary claims.

similarities: Both sources emphasize that claim level identifiers are crucial for 340B drugs to prevent duplicate discounts and manage rebate collection [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both the provider manual and policy update state that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources detail the exact NCPDP fields and their required values for submitting 340B drug claims to Medicaid, including SCC 20, BCD 08 for primary claims, 340B Acquisition Cost for Ingredient Cost, and Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers for U&C [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6].
differences: The provider manual specifies a $10.18 professional dispensing fee and notes that claims will pay at the pharmacys Usual and Customary pricing if lower, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs at their acquisition cost plus a professional dispensing fee, or the pharmacys usual and customary price if lower. To prevent duplicate discounts, all 340B claims must include specific claim level identifiers.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid requires 340B claim level identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts. These identifiers are mandatory at the claim submission level for all 340B drug claims. Their use ensures the claim is removed from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims must include claim level identifiers when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor. These identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care Plans, or as a secondary claim. This requirement applies to both medical and pharmacy claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at their 340B Acquisition Cost. A professional dispensing fee of $10.18 is added to this cost. However, claims will pay at the pharmacys Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if it is lower than the drug ingredient cost plus the dispensing fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid must be at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider. This cost should be net of any manufacturer discounts or other price reductions. This applies to both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees.

these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.

Pharmacy

A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Lower of NADAC or

SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs based on their 340B acquisition cost, along with a professional dispensing fee. To prevent duplicate discounts, all 340B claims must include specific claim-level identifiers, and the acquisition cost must be reported in the designated claim fields.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid requires 340B claim level identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts. These identifiers must be included at the claim submission level for all 340B drug claims. This process ensures the claim is removed from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug. This applies when billing to NYRx, a Medicaid Managed Care Plan, or as a secondary claim. The purpose is to ensure that rebates are not collected for these drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed based on their 340B Acquisition Cost. A Professional Dispensing Fee of $10.18 is added to this cost. This applies when the claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: All 340B-purchased drug claims submitted via NCPDP must report the actual acquisition cost. This requirement applies when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor. Covered entities are responsible for correctly identifying these claims.

similarities: Both sources require claim level identifiers on 340B drug claims to prevent duplicate discounts and ensure proper rebate processing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources state that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
differences: The policy guidance specifies that claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drugs billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care Plans, or as secondary claims, encompassing both medical and pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11]. The provider manual focuses on drug claims and their removal from rebate invoicing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at their 340B Acquisition Cost plus a $10.18 Professional Dispensing Fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy guidance specifies that NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims must be submitted at actual acquisition cost when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
answers.caveats: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current guidance on 340B drug reimbursement, while the New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 offers earlier policy details.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.

purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
