test_id_51.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
July 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Pharmacy
Reminder: Pharmacy Delivery Policy
Prescription and over-the-counter (OTC) drugs dispensed pursuant to a prescription or fiscal order submitted to
Medicaid for reimbursement for any portion, may be picked up at the pharmacy provider or may be delivered
free of charge to the Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) or Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) member’s home or
current residence including facilities and shelters.
Delivery Policy:
• All shipping and/or delivery costs shall be the responsibility of the provider of the service.
• The pharmacy is responsible for the delivery of product to the intended recipient.
• Prior to processing a drug claim, the pharmacy must confirm the drug is needed and that the drug had
not been discontinued, changed, or is no longer necessary (e.g., the member had changed pharmacy
provider); the confirmation must be maintained in the member’s patient record.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program covers home infusion drugs and supplies as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, not through a bundled payment. Pharmacies are responsible for billing, and must first bill Medicare or other insurance before Medicaid, which acts as the payer of last resort. Eligibility for pharmacy benefit coverage requires specific criteria, including NCPDP billing format and the drug not being included in a facilitys rate.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: It is the pharmacys responsibility to bill in a timely manner. Pharmacies must bill the appropriate party, which includes the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. This ensures proper payment for services rendered.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs are eligible for pharmacy benefit coverage under specific criteria. These drugs must be billable in the NCPDP format and delivered directly to the place of administration. Additionally, they must not be included in a facilitys rate and must be available from a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility must submit transactions using the NCPDP transaction format. This applies when the pharmacy benefit is not already included in the facilitys rate. The NCPDP format is essential for processing these claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after receiving payment information from Medicare.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: It is the pharmacys responsibility to bill in a timely manner. This includes billing the appropriate party, such as Medicare, before billing Medicaid. This establishes Medicaid as the payer of last resort.

similarities: Both documents indicate that pharmacies have a responsibility in the billing process for home infusion services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both the provider manual and the policy update specify that NCPDP transaction format is required for billing home infusion drugs when the pharmacy benefit is not included in a facilitys rate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both documents emphasize that pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid, establishing Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that home infusion treatments are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, a detail not present in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual lists additional eligibility criteria for pharmacy benefit coverage, such as direct delivery to the place of administration and availability from a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy, which are not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides more detailed instructions, stating that Medicaid can only be billed after payment information is received from Medicare, which is a specific operational detail not present in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.

Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.

including those billed as a secondary payment.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services; instead, these must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. Pharmacies are responsible for timely billing using the NCPDP transaction format for pharmacy benefit claims, while coverage for practitioner-administered drugs is based on FDA approval or recognized compendia.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit. Providers can access a list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: It is the pharmacys responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as a facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Pharmacies not required to bill a facility must submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program. This submission must use the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider are eligible for the pharmacy benefit if they meet specific criteria. These criteria include the drug being billable in the NCPDP format and deliverable directly to the place of administration. Additionally, the drug must not be included in a facility rate and must be available from a Medicaid-enrolled dispensing pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioners office. Coverage is for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications or where the medically accepted indication is supported in recognized compendia. This policy is broader as it focuses on clinical coverage requirements for practitioner-administered drugs in general, rather than specifically home infusion drugs under the pharmacy benefit.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance confirm that home infusion drugs, supplies, and services are not covered by a bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both documents address criteria for drugs administered by a practitioner, though with different focuses [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual outlines that home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, while the policy specifies the pharmacys responsibility for timely billing using the NCPDP transaction format for pharmacy benefit claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual details operational eligibility criteria for home infusion drugs under the pharmacy benefit, such as NCPDP billing capability, whereas the policy outlines clinical coverage requirements for practitioner-administered drugs, including FDA approval or support in recognized compendia [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The policy update for eligibility and coverage criteria is broader, covering general practitioner-administered drugs rather than specifically home infusion drugs under the pharmacy benefit, and is less recent than the provider manual guidance.

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs and supplies; these must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. Pharmacies are responsible for billing the appropriate party, including maximizing Medicare benefits first, and must adhere to specific criteria for coverage and billing formats.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as a facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Pharmacies not required to bill the facility, where the pharmacy benefit is not included in the rate, must submit transactions using the NCPDP format.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs requiring practitioner administration can be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits. For pharmacy benefit coverage, drugs must be billable in NCPDP format, deliverable to the place of administration, not included in a facility rate, and available from a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. Providers must also ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office for FDA-approved or medically accepted indications. Eligibility for reimbursement for certain drugs or drug classes is based on clinical criteria outlined on the DOH Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing Guidance web page.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid must participate in Medicare and maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both must first be billed to Medicare, and it is the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate Medicare benefit. Medicaid can only be billed after payment information is received from Medicare, using HCPCS or NDC as required by the Medicare Plan.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims after coordination of benefits with Third Party Liability (TPL) plans, including Medicare. Pharmacy providers must ensure claims with zero-fill reimbursement from TPL are adjudicated under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, using HCPCS or NDC.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate party, including Medicare, before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both the provider manual and policy indicate that claims submitted after Medicare or other Third Party Liability (TPL) must use appropriate coding, such as HCPCS or NDC [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual specifies detailed criteria for home infusion drugs to be covered under the pharmacy benefit, such as billability in NCPDP format and pharmacist counseling [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update, in contrast, focuses more broadly on general Fee-for-Service coverage for practitioner-administered drugs, outlining administration methods and reliance on DOH web page clinical criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid must participate in Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses on Medicaid paying patient responsibility after TPL coordination, including Medicare, and handling zero-fill claims [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

Providers, particularly pharmacies, are responsible for billing other payers like Medicare first, as Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. Providers can access a list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit online.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies not required to bill the facility, where the pharmacy benefit is not included in the rate, must submit transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program. These transactions must use the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. This policy is more specific about the required billing method for pharmacy benefits.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party. This includes billing the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. This policy statement is broader, encompassing timely billing and a wider range of potential payers.

similarities: Both sources indicate that home infusion drugs are not part of a bundled payment and require specific billing pathways [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both emphasize the pharmacys responsibility in billing for home infusion drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both imply that other payers, such as Medicare, should be billed before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifies that pharmacies not billing the facility must submit transactions through the online Pro-DUR program using the NCPDP transaction format [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual details the requirement to maximize Medicare benefits and bill Medicare first, only billing Medicaid after receiving Medicare payment information [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states the pharmacys responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.

Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid covers over-the-counter (OTC) naloxone when prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under a state-issued standing order, with pharmacies required to submit claims for reimbursement. All covered prescription drugs, including naloxone, are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis, with dispensing pharmacies billing directly to Medicaid for drugs on the List of Reimbursable Drugs.

aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid covers over-the-counter (OTC) naloxone. This coverage applies when naloxone is prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under a State-issued standing order. similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update indicate that pharmacies must submit claims for reimbursement of covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
Both sources imply that covered drugs, including naloxone, are subject to a reimbursement process involving direct billing by the dispensing pharmacy.
differences: The provider manual describes the general fee-for-service reimbursement framework for all prescription drugs, including the requirement for drugs to be on the List of Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifically addresses the coverage of over-the-counter naloxone and outlines the conditions for its dispensing and claiming, such as a practitioners prescription or a State-issued standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
The policy update provides specific operational details for naloxone dispensing that are not present in the broader guidance of the provider manual.
answers.caveats: The policy update provides specific details for over-the-counter naloxone coverage and dispensing, while the provider manual describes the general fee-for-service reimbursement for all prescription drugs, making the policy update more specific to the question of naloxone.

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.

nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

The drug is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the ordering prescriber's NPI, while administration is billed using procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy's NPI, receiving a specific administration fee.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Claims for the naloxone drug and its administration must be submitted separately. For the drug, the claim requires the ordering prescriber’s National Provider Identifier (NPI) and is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using standard methodology. For the administration, the claim must use procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy’s NPI and is reimbursed the current administration fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Naloxone drug and administration claims must be submitted separately. The drug claim requires the ordering prescriber’s National Provider Identifier (NPI) and is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using standard methodology. The administration claim must use procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy’s NPI and is reimbursed an administration fee of $13.36.

similarities: Both sources require separate claims for the naloxone drug and its administration [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both specify that the drug claim uses the ordering prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) and is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both state that the administration claim uses procedure code 96372 and the pharmacys National Provider Identifier (NPI) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The policy update specifies a fixed administration fee of $13.36, whereas the provider manual refers to the "current administration fee" without stating a specific amount [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective 2025-10-01, is more recent than the New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, effective 2024-04-01. Therefore, the "current administration fee" mentioned in the provider manual may supersede the specific amount stated in the older policy update.

Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone reimbursement involves separate billing for the drug and its administration, with the drug billed via NDC and administration via procedure code 96372. Reimbursement is contingent on the drug being on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs and dispensed under a valid order, such as a State-issued standing order for OTC naloxone.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If billing for both the naloxone drug and its administration, claims must be submitted separately. The drug is reimbursed when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology, with the ordering prescribers NPI. Administration is reimbursed at the current fee using procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy’s NPI.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed or dispensed in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order. A pharmacy must submit a claim and dispense OTC naloxone pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order. This policy statement is broader and less specific regarding the separate billing procedures for the drug and its administration.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. An exception exists for drugs provided by a facility that includes the cost in its all-inclusive rate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Only prescription and non-prescription drugs appearing on the New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, with their 11-digit NDCs, are reimbursable under NYRx. NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs unless they are on this list, except when provided by a facility with an all-inclusive rate.

similarities: Both sources confirm that naloxone reimbursement requires the drug to be on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources acknowledge an exception for drugs provided by facilities that include the cost in their all-inclusive rate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources indicate that naloxone can be dispensed under a standing order [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual specifies separate billing for the naloxone drug via NDC and its administration via procedure code 96372, including details on NPI usage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update confirms coverage for over-the-counter naloxone dispensed pursuant to a fiscal order or a State-issued standing order, but does not detail the specific billing codes for the drug and administration [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
The policy update explicitly mentions the requirement for 11-digit NDCs for reimbursable drugs under NYRx [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses for Over-the-Counter (OTC) naloxone when prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under a State-issued standing order, requiring pharmacies to submit a claim. While the provider manual outlines general drug claim submission requirements, it does not specifically detail naloxone reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Claims for drugs must be submitted using the ordering prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI). This NPI should be entered in the Prescriber ID field. Pharmacies receive reimbursement for medication when it is billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the established reimbursement methodology.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid covers over-the-counter (OTC) naloxone. This coverage applies when naloxone is prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under a State-issued standing order. Pharmacies must submit a claim for naloxone in compliance with New York State laws, regulations, and Medicaid policy.

similarities: Both the provider manual and the policy update require pharmacies to submit a claim for drug reimbursement.
Both documents address the general method for pharmacies to receive reimbursement for medications.
differences: The policy update specifically details the coverage conditions for over-the-counter naloxone, including when it is prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed via a State-issued standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual outlines general drug claim submission requirements, such as using the ordering prescribers NPI and billing via National Drug Code, without specific mention of naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The provider manual provides general guidance for drug claim submission and reimbursement, while the policy update specifically addresses naloxone coverage and claim submission requirements.
